title: Prism Protocol
compiled_truth:
Prism Protocol is a developer tools startup building an API schema validation and contract testing platform. Founded in 2025 by [Ora Hashimoto](people/ora-hashimoto), the company emerged from frustrations Ora experienced while managing microservice sprawl at a previous infrastructure role. The core product lets engineering teams define API contracts declaratively, then validates both producer and consumer sides against those contracts in CI/CD pipelines. It's a surprisingly sticky tool once adopted — teams that integrate Prism into their workflow rarely rip it out.

The company raised a seed round with backing from [Bex Andersen](people/bex-andersen), [Cal Suzuki](people/cal-suzuki), and [Eli Nakamura](people/eli-nakamura). Bex led the round and has been fairly hands-on, particularly around go-to-market positioning. Cal came in through a warm intro from Ora's previous network and has been quieter but strategic on the technical advisory side.

Prism Protocol's early traction was strongest among mid-size platform teams — companies with 20-80 engineers who had enough services to feel the pain of breaking changes but not enough tooling budget to build internal solutions. By mid-2027 the company had roughly 140 paying teams, mostly concentrated in fintech and healthtech verticals where API correctness carries regulatory weight.

The intersection with Anchor Codec's world is notable. In early 2027, Prism started exploring how deterministic data infrastructure could improve their validation guarantees. If the underlying data layer is deterministic, contract testing becomes significantly simpler — you can verify schema conformance at the transmission level rather than relying on application-layer assertions. Ora presented a prototype integration at a small meetup in Portland that caught some attention. There were subsequent conversations about whether Prism's contract definitions could be expressed as single-pass primitives, though nothing formal materialized until late 2027.

The team is lean — around 12 people as of early 2028, mostly engineers with a couple of developer advocates. Ora tends to run things with minimal process, which works at this size but has occasionally led to communication gaps. The product itself is well-regarded in its niche, though brand awareness outside of the developer tools community remians limited. Prism Protocol sits at an interesting inflection point: big enough to have real customers, small enough that one bad quarter could be existential.
timeline:
- **2025-11-10** | Prism Protocol incorporated by [Ora Hashimoto](people/ora-hashimoto) in Portland, Oregon.
- **2026-03-15** | Closed seed round led by [Bex Andersen](people/bex-andersen) with participation from [Cal Suzuki](people/cal-suzuki) and [Eli Nakamura](people/eli-nakamura).
- **2026-06-22** | Public beta launched; first 30 teams onboarded within six weeks.
- **2026-10-08** | Hired first developer advocate; began publishing technical content on contract testing patterns.
- **2027-02-14** | Reached 100 paying teams milestone. Ora posted a brief retrospective on the company blog.
- **2027-05-03** | Ora presented prototype integration with deterministic data infrastructure at a Portland developer meetup.
- **2027-08-19** | Informal exploratory meeting with Anchor Codec team to discuss single-pass validation primitives.
- **2027-11-30** | Shipped v2.0 of the platform with support for async event contracts and streaming APIs.
- **2028-01-22** | Team reached 12 people. Began scoping a potential enterprise tier targeting regulated industries.
- **2028-04-10** | [Eli Nakamura](people/eli-nakamura) joined a board observer seat as the company started planning its Series A process.

_facts.name: Prism Protocol
_facts.description: API schema validation and contract testing platform
